threats.title: PermissionsRegistry.removeRole Corrupts _addressToRoles Using Wrong Source Array for Swap-and-Pop
threats.hypothesis_description: In removeRole(), when cleaning up _addressToRoles for affected users, the code uses `_roles[_roles.length - 1]` (the last element of the GLOBAL roles array) instead of `_addressToRoles[rta[i]][_addressToRoles[rta[i]].length - 1]` (the last element of the user's role array) for the swap-and-pop operation. This corrupts users' role lists with unrelated role entries.
threats.investigation_rationale: The bug is in the swap-and-pop implementation which reads from the wrong array. Additionally, the inner loop doesn't break after pop, potentially causing further corruption. This code runs during normal admin operations (removeRole is an intended admin function).

proof_of_signal.pattern: _addressToRoles[rta[i]][k] = _roles[_roles.length -1] uses _roles (global) instead of _addressToRoles[rta[i]]

preconditions: blackMultisig calls removeRole for an existing role
At least one address has that role assigned

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Wrong source array in swap-and-pop: _roles instead of _addressToRoles[rta[i]]
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: After removeRole, affected addresses have corrupted role entries in _addressToRoles, potentially affecting subsequent removeRoleFrom operations
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No break after pop; wrong array reference

analysis_questions: Does the corruption in _addressToRoles affect hasRole checks?
Can subsequent removeRoleFrom calls behave incorrectly due to corrupted data?
Does the missing break after pop cause additional issues?
validation_requirements: Test removeRole with multiple addresses assigned, verify _addressToRoles integrity
Verify hasRole mapping is correctly updated (it uses a separate mapping)
Test removeRoleFrom after removeRole on affected addresses

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Admin calls removeRole('GAUGE_ADMIN'). For each address that had GAUGE_ADMIN, their _addressToRoles entry is replaced with the last element of global _roles (e.g., 'EPOCH_MANAGER'). The addressToRole view returns incorrect data, and subsequent removeRoleFrom operations may behave incorrectly or revert.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Moderate - role tracking corruption could prevent proper role management
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All addresses that had the removed role
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires manual cleanup of role assignments

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for removeRole with multiple assigned addresses

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by calling addressToRole() after removeRole

threats.title: SMNFT_BONUS Change Breaks totalSupply vs Individual Balance Invariant
threats.hypothesis_description: When team calls setSmNFTBonus, the global point's smNFTBonus is recalculated using calculate_sm_nft_bonus(smNFTBalance) with the NEW rate at every _checkpoint, but individual smNFT token checkpoints retain their OLD stored smNFTBonus values until individually re-checkpointed. This breaks the invariant that totalSupply == sum(balanceOfNFT) and causes incorrect reward distributions.
threats.investigation_rationale: In _checkpoint, `last_point.smNFTBonus = calculate_sm_nft_bonus(smNFTBalance)` uses the current SMNFT_BONUS. But each token's user_point_history stores smNFTBonus from creation time. After a bonus change, the global and individual views diverge.

proof_of_signal.pattern: last_point.smNFTBonus = calculate_sm_nft_bonus(smNFTBalance) uses current SMNFT_BONUS; individual checkpoints store fixed values

preconditions: smNFTs exist in the system
Team calls setSmNFTBonus to change the bonus rate

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Global checkpoint uses live calculate_sm_nft_bonus; individual checkpoints store historical values
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If bonus increases: totalSupply inflated relative to sum of individual balances. If decreases: totalSupply deflated. Both cause incorrect reward share calculations.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No migration mechanism to update existing smNFT checkpoints on bonus change

analysis_questions: How large is the divergence for realistic bonus changes?
Does the divergence self-correct when individual tokens are checkpointed?
Does RewardsDistributor use totalSupplyAtT for reward calculation?
validation_requirements: Create smNFTs, change bonus, compare totalSupply vs sum of balanceOfNFT
Verify RewardsDistributor._claim uses totalSupplyAtT in share calculation
Quantify reward distribution error

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: After team increases SMNFT_BONUS, totalSupply is inflated by the bonus increase on smNFTBalance but individual smNFT balances remain unchanged until re-checkpointed. Normal lock holders receive less than their fair share of rebase rewards because supply denominator is artificially high.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Incorrect rebase reward distribution to all users
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All veNFT holders (diluted if bonus increases)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Partially recoverable by individually checkpointing all smNFTs

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for SMNFT_BONUS change impact on existing locks

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare totalSupply vs sum of individual balanceOfNFT

threats.title: VoterV3 voted Flag Stuck True When All Voted Gauges Are Killed, Blocking Token Transfers
threats.hypothesis_description: When poke() or vote() is called and all previously voted gauges are killed, _vote() calls _reset() (clearing weights) but only calls IVotingEscrow(_ve).voting(_tokenId) when _usedWeight > 0. Since all gauges are dead, _usedWeight = 0 and voting() is not called. However, the voted flag remains true from the previous vote, preventing transfers and withdrawals until the user calls reset() in the next epoch.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _vote function sets voted=true only when _usedWeight > 0, but doesn't set it to false when _usedWeight = 0. Since poke() doesn't have onlyNewEpoch modifier restrictions, this state can be reached within the current epoch.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (_usedWeight > 0) IVotingEscrow(_ve).voting(_tokenId); — voted flag not cleared when _usedWeight == 0

preconditions: User has active votes on gauges
All voted gauges are killed
poke() is called (e.g., via deposit_for or increase_amount)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: voted flag only set to true when _usedWeight > 0; abstain only called in explicit reset()
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: After poke with all dead gauges: usedWeights=0 but voted=true. Token cannot be transferred or withdrawn.

analysis_questions: Can this be triggered via deposit_for which calls poke when voted?
How long is the user locked (until next epoch's reset window)?
Can this be weaponized to prevent emergency withdrawals?
validation_requirements: Test: vote for gauge, kill gauge, call deposit_for, verify transfer blocked
Verify reset() in next epoch properly clears voted flag
Check if there's any other way to clear voted flag

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A governance actor kills all gauges that a target user has voted for. When poke is triggered (via deposit_for or increase_amount), the user's voted flag stays true but usedWeights becomes 0. The user cannot transfer or withdraw their veNFT until the next epoch when they can call reset().

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Temporary lock of user funds (one epoch duration)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Automatic recovery next epoch via reset()

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for gauge killing impact on voted flag consistency

threats.title: AutoVotingEscrow.lockOwner Returns Incorrect Owner for Non-Existent or Released Token IDs
threats.hypothesis_description: lockOwner() uses tokenIdIndex mapping which defaults to 0 for non-existent tokenIds. If locks array is non-empty, lockOwner returns locks[0].owner for any tokenId not in the AVM, including released tokens whose index was deleted (reset to 0). The require check `index < locks.length` passes when locks is non-empty.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function lacks a validation that locks[index].tokenId == _tokenId, meaning the returned owner may belong to a completely different token. While the manager's tokenIdToAVMId check provides a guard, any direct call to lockOwner bypasses this.

proof_of_signal.pattern: tokenIdIndex defaults to 0; no validation that locks[index].tokenId == _tokenId

preconditions: At least one lock exists in the AutoVotingEscrow
lockOwner is called with a tokenId that was never added or was previously released

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: getOriginalOwner calls lockOwner, potentially returning wrong owner for reward routing

Test getOriginalOwner for non-AVM tokenIds
Check all callers of lockOwner across the codebase

prerequisites: At least one lock in the AutoVotingEscrow

threats.title: Gas-bounded checkpoint logic may permanently desync voting power after ~5.3 days inactivity (WEEK=1800), breaking totalSupply/vote accounting
threats.hypothesis_description: VotingEscrow._checkpoint() advances global point history in a loop capped to 255 iterations, each step advancing by WEEK. With WEEK=1800 seconds, a single call can only catch up ~5.3 days. If the system goes longer without successful checkpointing, global history cannot be fully updated in one call, potentially causing persistent mis-accounting in totalSupplyAtT/totalSupplyAt and downstream reward distribution calculations.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a deterministic, on-chain reachable correctness failure that can occur naturally (low activity) or be induced by grief (discourage checkpointing). It affects core invariants around total voting power and can impact emissions/rebases/claims that depend on historical supply.

inventory_refs.function: totalSupplyAtT(uint t, uint epoch, mapping(uint => int128) storage slope_changes, Data storage VotingBalanceLogicData)

proof_of_signal.pattern: _checkpoint() for-loop `for (uint i = 0; i < 255; ++i)` advancing `t_i += WEEK` where WEEK = 1800 (BlackTimeLibrary)

preconditions: No successful checkpointing interaction (deposit/withdraw/merge/split/checkpoint) occurs for > 255*WEEK seconds
Protocol continues to rely on totalSupplyAtT/totalSupplyAt for rewards/governance
At least one state-changing operation later calls _checkpoint() expecting it to fully catch up

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: bounded historical catch-up loop with extremely short epoch size
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: point_history may stop being updated to current timestamp, making slope/bias schedule application incomplete
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no mechanism to iterate across multiple calls atomically or to enforce periodic checkpointing

analysis_questions: If gap exceeds 255*WEEK, does _checkpoint stop early leaving last_point.ts < block.timestamp?
Do subsequent calls eventually catch up by repeated interactions, or does the design require full catch-up in a single call to remain correct?
Which core operations (RewardsDistributor claim math, governance snapshots) become wrong or revert as a result?
validation_requirements: Simulate time jump >6 days and execute checkpoint; assert point_history[epoch].ts == block.timestamp (or expected)
Check totalSupplyAtT monotonicity and consistency pre/post gap
Assess downstream impact on RewardsDistributor calculations and voting

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Protocol experiences low activity for >6 days; the first user interaction afterward cannot fully update the global checkpoints due to the 255-iteration bound, leading to incorrect total supply and potentially misallocated/disrupted reward calculations for many users.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: High systemic risk: mispriced supply affects reward distribution (rebases) and could distort governance and emissions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Potentially all veNFT holders and reward recipients
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: May require repeated calls, manual maintenance transactions, or contract upgrade; historical accuracy may be compromised

threats.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing long-gap time travel tests given WEEK=1800

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor point_history[epoch].ts lag vs block.timestamp and mismatches in totalSupplyAtT

threats.title: Delegation Checkpoint Wipe-Out via Single Token Transfer
threats.hypothesis_description: In VotingDelegationLib.moveTokenDelegates, the source delegate filtering uses `ownerOfFn(tId) == srcRep` to decide which tokens to keep. When a user who has delegated to address C transfers a single token, ALL tokens delegated to C by ANY user whose tokens are not owned by C are wiped from C's checkpoint. An attacker can wipe a major delegate's governance voting power by creating a minimal lock, delegating to the target, then transferring that token away.
threats.investigation_rationale: The filtering condition is fundamentally wrong: it retains tokens where ownerOf(tId) == delegate_address, but delegates rarely OWN the tokens delegated to them. This means on any transfer by a delegator, ALL other delegators' tokens are also removed from the delegate's checkpoint. This is a direct governance manipulation vector.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ownerOfFn(tId) == srcRep used to filter tokens to keep; should be tId != _tokenId. When srcRep is a delegate (not the owner), no owned tokens match, wiping all entries.

preconditions: Target delegate C has voting power delegated from at least one other user
Attacker can create a minimal veNFT lock
Attacker calls delegate(C) then transferFrom to a second address

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: ownerOfFn(tId) == srcRep — filters by ownership not token identity
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Create lock → delegate to target → transfer token. All other delegators' tokens removed from target's checkpoint
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker wipes governance voting power of a major delegate, enabling hostile governance proposals to pass or preventing quorum

threats.title: delegateBySig Replay Attack Due to Nonce Not Incremented by Direct delegate()
threats.hypothesis_description: The delegate() function does not increment the nonces mapping, so previously signed delegateBySig messages remain valid after a user calls delegate() directly. An attacker who received a valid delegation signature can submit it at any time to override the signatory's subsequent direct delegate() calls, redirecting all of the victim's veNFT governance voting power.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a standard EIP-712 nonce management bug. The nonce is only consumed by delegateBySig but not invalidated by delegate(). Since delegation controls governance voting power, this allows stale signatures to override current delegation preferences.

proof_of_signal.pattern: delegate() calls _delegate() but never increments nonces[msg.sender]. delegateBySig at line 627-657 checks nonce == nonces[signatory]++. Old signatures remain valid after direct delegate() calls.

preconditions: Victim has previously signed a delegateBySig message (e.g., for gasless delegation)
Attacker (or intended delegatee) possesses the signed message
Victim subsequently changed delegation via direct delegate() call

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: delegate() does not increment nonces[]; nonces[signatory]++ only in delegateBySig
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Submit old delegation signature to override victim's current delegation, redirecting governance voting power
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Control victim's governance votes to pass/block proposals, potentially affecting treasury management

threats.title: Expired Lock Merge into SMNFT Grants Free Bonus Voting Power
threats.hypothesis_description: The merge function does not check that the source token has an active lock for non-permanent sources. An expired non-permanent lock can be merged into an SMNFT target, burning the expired tokens and granting SMNFT bonus voting power (10% by default). The user effectively converts withdrawable tokens into permanent voting power with a free bonus, when the intent is that SMNFT conversions require commitment (non-expired tokens).
threats.investigation_rationale: The merge function only checks `_locked1.end > block.timestamp || _locked1.isPermanent` for the destination, not the source. Users can merge expired locks to gain bonus voting power without the intended time-lock commitment.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Only _locked1 (destination) checked for expiration: require(_locked1.end > block.timestamp || _locked1.isPermanent). No corresponding check for _locked0 (source) when !_locked0.isPermanent.

preconditions: User owns an expired normal veNFT (source) and an SMNFT (destination)
Source lock not voted or attached
SMNFT_BONUS > 0 (default 1000/10000 = 10%)

threats.title: SMNFT Split Does Not Update smNFTBalance Causing Global Accounting Divergence
threats.hypothesis_description: When splitting an SMNFT, the split function does not adjust smNFTBalance. The original SMNFT is burned and two new ones are created, but the global smNFTBalance tracker remains unchanged. While this is correct if no bonus rate changes occur, if SMNFT_BONUS changes between creation and split, the two new tokens' calculate_original_sm_nft_amount results using the current rate will sum to a different value than the original's real amount, breaking INV-STATE-006.
threats.investigation_rationale: The split function uses current SMNFT_BONUS to calculate _splitAmount for SMNFTs. If bonus changed since creation, the split amounts are calculated with the new rate, creating tokens whose 'original amounts' (via calculate_original_sm_nft_amount) don't sum to the original's original amount. smNFTBalance becomes inconsistent with actual holdings.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _splitAmount = int128(int256(_amount + calculate_sm_nft_bonus(_amount))) uses current SMNFT_BONUS; smNFTBalance is never adjusted in split

preconditions: SMNFT_BONUS has been changed via setSmNFTBonus since the SMNFT was created
canSplit[msg.sender] or canSplit[address(0)] is true
User splits an SMNFT

threats.title: AVM-Delegated veNFT Bribe Rewards Permanently Locked in AutoVotingEscrow Contracts
threats.hypothesis_description: Bribe.getReward() sends rewards to ownerOf(tokenId) which returns the AutoVotingEscrow (AVE) instance address for AVM-managed tokens. The check `_owner == avm` compares the AVE address against the AutoVotingEscrowManager address — always false. Rewards are transferred to the AVE contract which has zero ERC20 recovery functions. Additionally, setOriginalOwner in the manager is a no-op (empty body), and VotingEscrow._transferFrom's `_to == avm` check never triggers because the actual transfer destination is the AVE instance, not the manager. All bribe rewards for AVM-managed tokens are permanently lost.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a double-layered integration failure affecting every user who enables auto-voting. The AVE address != AVM manager address comparison guarantees the wrong code path. Even if patched, the no-op setOriginalOwner provides no fallback.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ownerOf returns AVE address; `_owner == avm` compares AVE vs Manager (always false); safeTransfer sends to AVE with no recovery

preconditions: User has enabled auto-voting for at least one veNFT
The AVM-managed veNFT has voted for pools with bribe rewards
gaugeManager calls getReward for the tokenId

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: AutoVotingEscrow holds NFTs but has no ERC20 withdrawal. Bribe.getReward sends tokens to ownerOf which is the AVE.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: setOriginalOwner is empty (line ~248 in AutoVotingEscrowManager); no admin action can fix already-locked funds
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — every bribe claim for AVM tokens triggers this loss

threats.title: Bribe Reward Claims Permanently DoS'd by Unbounded earned() Loop with WEEK=1800
threats.hypothesis_description: The Bribe.earned() function iterates over every epoch since lastEarn with no iteration cap. With WEEK=1800 seconds (30 minutes), epochs accumulate at 48/day. After ~8-10 real-world days without claiming, earned() requires 384-480 iterations, each performing two binary searches over storage (getPriorBalanceIndex + getPriorSupplyIndex). At ~39,000 gas per iteration, this exceeds Avalanche's 15M block gas limit. Since lastEarn is only updated inside getReward() which calls earned(), the DoS is self-reinforcing and permanent.
threats.investigation_rationale: The combination of WEEK=1800 (extremely short epochs for production) and unbounded iteration creates a practical gas DoS after just days of inactivity. This affects any user who doesn't claim every few days.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint256 i = 0; i < numEpochs; i++) loop with no upper bound; numEpochs = (epochStart(now) - lastEarnEpoch) / WEEK; WEEK=1800

preconditions: WEEK = 1800 seconds (confirmed in BlackTimeLibrary)
User hasn't claimed bribe rewards for ~8-10 days
Block gas limit is 15M (Avalanche C-chain)

threats.title: Last-Hour Voting Restriction Bypass via deposit_for Triggering Poke for Any Voted Token
threats.hypothesis_description: VoterV3.vote() restricts voting in the last hour of an epoch (epochVoteEnd check) for non-whitelisted, non-AVM tokens. However, VoterV3.poke() lacks this restriction — it only checks epochVoteStart. VotingEscrow.deposit_for() has no authentication requirement and triggers poke() for any voted token. Since msg.sender for poke() is the VE contract (passes `msg.sender == _ve` check), anyone can force a vote weight recalculation during the restricted last hour by depositing 1 wei into any voted token.
threats.investigation_rationale: The epochVoteEnd restriction was designed to prevent last-minute vote sniping. The deposit_for→poke path completely bypasses this restriction, allowing vote weight manipulation after the voting window closes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: poke() only checks `_timestamp <= epochVoteStart` (line 139), NOT epochVoteEnd; contrast vote() which checks `_timestamp >= epochVoteEnd` (line 161)

threats.title: Division-by-zero DoS in VoterV3 `_vote` when all selected gauges are dead (kills voting/reset workflows)
threats.hypothesis_description: In VoterV3._vote, `_totalVoteWeight` is computed by summing `_weights[i]` only for pools whose gauges are alive. If all selected pools are dead, `_totalVoteWeight` remains 0, and the subsequent computation `_poolWeight = _weights[i] * _weight / _totalVoteWeight` will revert if any pool is alive-check-passed later (or can still revert depending on compiler behavior on division by zero if code path is reached). This can block voting and poke flows for affected tokenIds, potentially trapping `voted` state or preventing normal epoch participation.
threats.investigation_rationale: Killing gauges is a plausible operational event (admin/governance). Users frequently vote/poke; if the function reverts under common conditions, it becomes an availability risk and can be weaponized by selecting dead pools.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _totalVoteWeight sums only alive gauges; later `_poolWeight = _weights[i] * _weight / _totalVoteWeight` with no guard for `_totalVoteWeight==0`

threats.title: disableAutoVoting Permanent Lockout When AVM Has Already Voted in Current Epoch
threats.hypothesis_description: disableAutoVoting calls IVoter(voter).reset(tokenId) which has the onlyNewEpoch modifier. If the AVM has already voted for this tokenId in the current epoch, lastVoted[tokenId] >= epochStart(now), causing reset to revert with 'VOTED'. Additionally, the voted flag prevents transferFrom. Users cannot exit AVM until the next epoch, and with WEEK=1800, the next epoch window is the 1200-second non-last-hour window of the next 30-minute epoch.
threats.investigation_rationale: The AVM votes in the last hour of each epoch via performLockAction. After voting, the user cannot disable auto-voting until the next epoch. With short epochs, this creates a tight window where users can exit, and if they miss it, they're locked for another full epoch.

proof_of_signal.pattern: disableAutoVoting calls IVoter(voter).reset(tokenId) which has onlyNewEpoch modifier checking lastVoted[tokenId]. AVM sets lastVoted when voting via voteLocks -> voter.vote.

preconditions: User has enabled auto-voting for their tokenId
AVM executor has already called performLockAction with isVote=true for this epoch
User attempts to disable auto-voting in the same epoch

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: lastVoted[tokenId] = epochStart(now) + 1 after AVM votes. onlyNewEpoch requires epochStart(now) > lastVoted. Until next epoch starts, this check fails.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Not an attacker-driven exploit, but a design flaw that traps users. Malicious executor could vote early in the 'last hour' window to maximize lockout duration.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Locked users cannot participate in alternative voting strategies or exit during volatile market conditions

economic_questions: What is the maximum time a user can be locked in AVM?
Can the executor strategically time votes to maximize user lockout?
Is there any emergency exit mechanism?
validation_requirements: Confirm disableAutoVoting reverts after AVM has voted in same epoch
Calculate lockout duration: from AVM vote time to next epoch's non-last-hour window
Verify no alternative exit path exists

preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Up to ~1800 seconds (one epoch) per lockout

threats.title: AutoVotingEscrow.voteLocks Claims Rebases for All Managed Tokens Without Access Control on claim()
threats.hypothesis_description: AutoVotingEscrow.voteLocks calls RewardsDistributor.claim(tokenId) for every active lock before voting. RewardsDistributor.claim() has no access control - anyone can call it for any tokenId. For active locks, claim() compounds rewards via deposit_for. This means the AVM executor determines WHEN rebases are compounded for all AVM-managed tokens, and any external caller can also force-compound. This could be weaponized to force-compound rebases at unfavorable times or to trigger poke side effects.
threats.investigation_rationale: The claim -> deposit_for -> poke chain means claiming rebases for an active lock increases its locked amount AND updates its bribe deposit. An attacker could call claim() on a target's tokenId right before voting to change the distribution of bribe rewards.

proof_of_signal.pattern: voteLocks calls IRewardsDistributor(rewardsDistributor).claim(tokenId) before IVoter(voter).vote(). claim() has no access control and triggers deposit_for which triggers poke

preconditions: Token has accumulated rebase rewards in RewardsDistributor
Token is AVM-managed with active lock (end > now or isPermanent)
claim() calls deposit_for which triggers poke if voted

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: claim -> deposit_for -> poke chain updates bribe deposits. Amount compounded depends on accumulated rebases and timing.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Anyone can call RewardsDistributor.claim(targetTokenId) to force-compound and trigger poke, changing the target's bribe deposit weight at a specific time
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Strategic timing of force-compound could be used to dilute or concentrate bribe rewards

economic_questions: How much voting power change does a typical rebase claim cause?
Can the timing of force-compound materially affect bribe distributions?
Does the poke chain always execute when deposit_for is called from claim?
validation_requirements: Verify claim -> deposit_for -> poke chain for AVM-managed tokens
Calculate typical rebase amount vs existing lock amount (% change)
Test whether force-compounding significantly shifts bribe distributions

threats.title: BlackClaims recoverERC20 Can Drain Active Season Reward Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The recoverERC20() function allows the owner to withdraw ANY ERC20 token, including the reward token (BLACK), from the contract. During an active claiming season, calling recoverERC20(address(token)) would drain the contract's reward balance, causing subsequent claimAndStakeReward calls to fail due to insufficient balance, without updating remaining_reward_amount.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unlike emergencyRecoverERC20 in Bribe.sol which is documented as dangerous, BlackClaims.recoverERC20 has no exclusion of the reward token and no accounting adjustment. The contract's dev comment suggests it's for recovering accidentally sent tokens, but it can drain active season funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No check that tokenAddress_ != address(token); transfers full balance to msg.sender

preconditions: Owner calls recoverERC20 with the reward token address
Season is finalized with remaining_reward_amount > 0

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing require(tokenAddress_ != address(token)) or accounting adjustment

Does the owner role have other mechanisms to drain funds?

Check if revokeUnclaimedReward is the intended recovery path

threats.title: PermissionsRegistry.removeRole may corrupt address role lists, breaking downstream authorization tooling and role revocation workflows
threats.hypothesis_description: When removing a role, the cleanup loop that edits _addressToRoles appears to use the global _roles array for swap-and-pop rather than the per-address role array, potentially writing an unrelated role into a user's role list. While hasRole is cleared, the view-based role enumeration becomes inconsistent and can cause operational errors (e.g., failing to revoke expected roles or misreporting permissions).
threats.investigation_rationale: Role registries are trust roots. Even if on-chain modifiers use hasRole mapping, corrupted enumeration impacts incident response, monitoring, and any contracts/off-chain systems that depend on addressToRole() for audits or revocation scripts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: removeRole(): `_addressToRoles[rta[i]][k] = _roles[_roles.length -1]; _addressToRoles[rta[i]].pop();` uses global _roles array element, not per-address last element

preconditions: blackMultisig executes removeRole(role)
At least one address had that role and also has other roles
Operational tooling relies on addressToRole() or _addressToRoles for remediation

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: address role lists become inconsistent with hasRole mapping and reality
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no invariant checks ensuring (role in address list) iff hasRole[role][addr]

threats.hypothesis_description: PermissionsRegistry.removeRole uses an incorrect swap source when cleaning `_addressToRoles`, writing a role from the global `_roles` array into a user’s role list instead of swapping within that user’s role list. threats.title: Bribe.recoverERC20AndUpdateData Subtracts from Wrong Epoch Due to Stale active_period
threats.hypothesis_description: recoverERC20AndUpdateData uses IMinter(minter).active_period() + WEEK to determine which epoch's rewards to subtract from. If the minter's active_period hasn't been updated (e.g., epoch flip hasn't occurred), this targets the wrong epoch. The subtraction _lastReward - tokenAmount can also underflow if tokenAmount exceeds rewards recorded for that epoch, reverting the admin recovery operation or corrupting reward data for the wrong epoch.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function assumes active_period() returns the current epoch, but active_period is only updated when update_period() is called on the minter. Between epoch flips, active_period may be stale, pointing to a previous epoch's rewards.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 _startTimestamp = IMinter(minter).active_period() + WEEK; tokenRewardsPerEpoch[tokenAddress][_startTimestamp] = _lastReward - tokenAmount; Epoch targeting depends on minter state

preconditions: Admin calls recoverERC20AndUpdateData (onlyAllowed)
Minter's active_period is stale (update_period not recently called)
tokenAmount may exceed rewards at the calculated epoch

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Indirect: corrupted reward data could deny rewards to legitimate claimers or make specific epochs' rewards permanently locked

threats.title: PermissionsRegistry.removeRoleFrom Loop Fails to Break After Swap-Pop Causing Double Removal
threats.hypothesis_description: In removeRoleFrom, the swap-and-pop loops for both _roleToAddresses and _addressToRoles do not break after finding and removing the target element. If the swapped-in last element happens to match the search condition, the loop will pop it too, removing TWO entries instead of one. This corrupts the permission tracking arrays and could cause addresses to lose role assignments they should retain.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function iterates over arrays, performs swap-and-pop when it finds a match, but continues iterating. After the swap, the last element is now at position i. If the next iteration checks this swapped element and it matches (which can happen in _addressToRoles if the user has duplicate-keyed roles from prior corruption via removeRole bug), a second pop occurs.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Two swap-and-pop loops without break statements. If swapped-in element matches condition, double-pop occurs: rta[i] = rta[rta.length-1]; rta.pop(); and loop continues checking position i again.

preconditions: Caller is blackMultisig
The last element in _roleToAddresses or _addressToRoles matches the removal target after swap
Prior removeRole corruption has created duplicate entries in _addressToRoles

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: After swap: rta[i] = rta[length-1]; rta.pop(). If rta[i] (now the swapped element) == target, next iteration pops again. In _roleToAddresses, if last address == c, double-removal occurs.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin removing a role from an address inadvertently removes another address's role assignment
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Corrupted permissions could allow unauthorized access or deny legitimate access to critical protocol functions
